skippedSources: Live video with Maggie Lake (Substack live-stream notification) — automated announcement only, zero sentences of written content
Lead-Lag Report webinar invite (Jason Hsu / Factor Investing, July 16) — sponsored event promo, zero market analysis
Cubic Analytics (Price Is What Pays) — pure subscription discount pitch, zero data or thesis content
TheStreet Daily (Earnings season looms) — bare headline roundup; the one substantive claim (a Wells Fargo report on AI's cost to consumers) had no retrievable article link in the email body

items.title: Core PCE is tracking 1.9% SAAR — already back at target
items.idea: RenMac's inputs-based model, using June CPI and PPI, estimates core PCE inflation rose 0.16% m/m in June, which annualizes to roughly 1.9% SAAR — technically back at the Fed's 2% target. Yet the market spent the same week debating whether the Fed hikes, not cuts.
items.angle: If the actual inflation gauge the Fed targets is already near 2%, the hike debate isn't really about inflation anymore — it's a proxy argument about oil and the Strait of Hormuz.
items.tweetA: Core PCE is tracking 1.9% SAAR by RenMac's model — that is AT target. And the market spent this week debating a HIKE. This stopped being an inflation argument days ago; it's an oil-shock hedge wearing an inflation costume.
items.tweetB: Every Fed-hike argument this week cites CPI or PPI. Nobody's citing the actual PCE estimate, which is sitting at target. Watch which data point the hawks quietly stop mentioning first.

charts.desc: RenMac chart of the CPI/PPI-implied core PCE path embedded in the issue

items.source: Gavekal via John Authers (Bloomberg) / Daily Chartbook (DC Lite #640)
items.title: Bank deposits are outgrowing loans — the opposite of a credit crunch
items.idea: Gavekal's data shows 2026 deposit growth at US banks outpacing the expansion of both commercial lending and securities holdings, which Authers reads as evidence banks have spare capacity to extend more credit, not less.
items.angle: This cuts against the hyperscaler-bond-stress narrative running through several other newsletters today (Goldman, SpaceX's bond) — the banking system isn't the source of credit strain right now; the corporate bond market is.
items.tweetA: Bank deposits are outgrowing loan books in 2026. Same week Goldman is warning hyperscaler bond issuance is straining credit markets. Two different credit markets, two completely different moods — and only one of them is actually stressed.
items.tweetB: If you're trading the AI-capex-funding-crunch thesis, know which market you mean. Banks have room. Corporate bond investors are the ones getting pickier.

charts.desc: Gavekal chart of deposit growth vs loan/securities growth

items.source: Chris Williamson, S&P Global / Daily Chartbook (DC Lite #640)
items.title: Fund managers are hoarding risk aversion right when retail is maxing out leverage
items.idea: S&P Global's Investment Manager Index shows risk appetite still muted, with concerns concentrated on valuations, geopolitics, and monetary policy — the same three things dominating every other headline this week.
items.angle: A muted institutional risk survey landing the same week as a 40%+ leveraged-ETF blowup in Korea and a chip-sector rout is a positioning mismatch worth tracking, not a coincidence.
items.tweetA: S&P Global's fund manager survey: risk appetite muted, worries are valuations, geopolitics, monetary policy. That's not a summary of the survey. That's just this week's news.
items.tweetB: Institutional risk appetite is muted at the exact moment Korean retail leverage and IPO froth are loudest. That gap between who's scared and who's levered up doesn't usually last long.

charts.desc: S&P Global IMI chart embedded in the issue

items.title: Only the 4th time this bull market SPX stalled this close to a new high without hitting one
items.idea: This is only the 4th time in this bull market that the S&P 500 has sat within 5% of its all-time high without printing a new one. Excluding the 2024 instance, every prior case was followed by a real pullback.
items.angle: Pair this with the chip-sector rout happening the same week — the market's stalled-out feel isn't noise, it's a historically reliable stall pattern showing up again.
items.tweetA: 3 of the last 4 times SPX stalled within 5% of an all-time high without breaking it, a real pullback followed. We're inside stall number 4 right now, during the worst week for semis in months. That's not a coincidence.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the chip rout for the tape's next move. Nobody's watching the no-new-high streak. They're both saying the same thing.

charts.desc: Chart of SPX distance from ATH across the 4 stall instances this bull market — clean historical pattern, tweetable on its own

items.title: S&P has been above its 200-day average for 300 straight sessions — that's happened 14 times since 1975
items.idea: Since 1975, a 300-day streak above the 200-day moving average has occurred only 14 other times; the forward 12-month return profile after those streaks runs well below the index's historical average, per Daily Chartbook's backtest dashboard.
items.angle: This is the same late-cycle, priced-for-perfection tell running through today's BlackRock AUM highs and SpaceX IPO froth — long uptrend streaks don't reward chasing, historically.
items.tweetA: 300 straight days above the 200-day average. Only 14 other times since 1975. Forward 12-month returns after those streaks: below average, every single time in the study. Streaks like this don't usually reward buying more of the streak.
items.tweetB: Either this 300-day streak really is different, or the market is doing exactly what it did the other 14 times this streak formed — grinding out one more leg before mean reversion shows up.

charts.desc: Daily Chartbook Studies backtest dashboard chart, 300-day streak instances and forward returns

items.source: ARK Invest / ARK Second Quarter ETF Report
items.title: ARK says CPI and PCE are lying to you because they can't measure AI deflation
items.idea: ARK argues CPI- and PCE-based inflation gauges are overstating true inflation because AI, robotics, energy storage, multiomics, and public blockchains are structurally deflationary, compressing the cost of intelligence, energy, and biological discovery faster than backward-looking price indices can capture.
items.angle: Convenient thesis for a fund family holding a portfolio of unprofitable growth names that need low rates to work — worth weighing against today's actual PPI miss, which was almost entirely an energy-price artifact, not a robotics one.
items.tweetA: ARK's argument: inflation gauges are wrong because they can't see AI deflation. Convenient thesis for a firm holding a book of long-duration growth stocks that need low rates to justify their multiples.
items.tweetB: If AI deflation were really running as hot as ARK claims, PPI should be undershooting consensus every single month, not only when energy prices happen to fall. This week's miss was oil, not robots.

charts.desc: No chart in email; marketing-style ETF report announcement

items.title: China is buying the gold dip the West is selling
items.idea: Gold fell from roughly $5,400/oz in early March to about $4,030 by mid-June on repriced Fed hike odds, but Chinese investors bought a record 210.7 tonnes of bars and coins in Q1 and Chinese gold imports rose 76% y/y through May, while Western ETFs sold 78 tonnes YTD.
items.angle: The real gold signal right now isn't the dollar or the rate path, it's the China-vs-West divergence: physical accumulation in Asia against paper liquidation in the West.
items.tweetA: China bought a record 210.7 tonnes of gold bars and coins in Q1. Western ETFs sold 78 tonnes YTD. Same market, opposite conviction. Guess which side has been right about currency debasement so far.
items.tweetB: Gold isn't broken, it's being repriced through a hostile rate window. BNP's framing: the paper market is lightening its load while the physical market is quietly stacking cargo. That's a structure, not a top.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the gold price path and China import data

items.title: China just wrote a $9.8B check to break the memory oligopoly
items.idea: ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) is preparing a $9.8B Shanghai listing to fund DRAM capacity expansion; its market share has already doubled to roughly 8% in a year, with H1 revenue guidance of 120B yuan, and Apple is reportedly in talks to buy CXMT chips.
items.angle: The entire memory-supercycle bull case rests on Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron holding ~90% of DRAM with disciplined pricing — a state-backed fourth supplier built specifically to break that discipline is the risk nobody's pricing into memory-stock multiples.
items.tweetA: The whole memory bull case assumes Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron keep supply discipline. China just wrote a $9.8B check to make sure they can't. $MU
items.tweetB: Same week: CXMT raises $9.8B to flood the DRAM market, and Samsung reports its own phone division's first loss ever because memory costs are eating its margins. The oligopoly is getting squeezed from both sides at once.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for CXMT market share and revenue trajectory

items.title: Korea's leveraged chip ETFs arrived at the top and are down 40% since
items.idea: Seoul's single-stock leveraged ETFs on SK Hynix and Samsung launched in late May and pulled in roughly $9.1B within a month; the largest SK Hynix leveraged ETF is down more than 40% since launch, and the two stocks plus their leveraged products now make up ~73% of Korean equity turnover, up from 31% before launch.
items.angle: A textbook case of leverage amplifying volatility right as institutional risk appetite (see the muted IMI survey above) was already pulling back — retail chased the hottest trade in the market at the exact wrong moment.
items.tweetA: A leveraged SK Hynix ETF is down more than 40% in under two months. Two stocks plus their leveraged wrappers are now 73% of Korean equity turnover. That's not a stock market anymore, that's one bet with extra steps.
items.tweetB: Korean retail got access to 2x semiconductor leverage at literally the top of the move. Daily rebalancing decay plus concentration risk plus a momentum unwind — this is the textbook case for why regulators gate these products in the first place.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the leveraged ETF drawdown and turnover-concentration data

items.title: Fed hike odds fell from 71% to 51% on data that's already stale
items.idea: June CPI came in at 3.5% annualized (vs 3.8% expected) and June PPI at 5.5% annualized (vs expectations), both driven almost entirely by falling June energy prices that have already reversed in July; the beat pulled 2026 Fed hike odds down from a Monday peak of 71% to around 51%, while gold compresses into a triangle pattern at its $3,900-$4,100 support zone.
items.angle: An inflation miss that's purely an energy-price mirage, reversing in real time as Hormuz reopens the risk premium, means the hike-odds unwind driving this week's rally may be trading on data that's already out of date.
items.tweetA: Fed hike odds went from 71% to 51% off a CPI/PPI beat that's basically entirely an energy-price artifact. Oil's already back up in July. This entire repricing might be trading on last month's weather, not this month's.
items.tweetB: Gold is compressed into a triangle at $3,900-$4,100 support. A move built on stale June data, already reversed by July's oil spike, is exactly the setup that resolves violently once the market catches up to itself.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the gold support/resistance triangle chart

items.title: The Pentagon just turned a hormone level into an annual readiness KPI
items.idea: Defense Secretary Hegseth ordered annual testosterone-deficiency screening for all troops age 30+ as part of the existing Periodic Health Assessment, with elective TRT offered to those who screen low; the Endocrine Society requires two confirmed morning tests before diagnosing hypogonadism, and the FDA has previously flagged cardiovascular risk from TRT prescribed for age-related decline alone.
items.angle: A single annual screen, layered on a biomarker that swings with sleep, illness, and time of day, is built to generate referrals and a chronic-care tail across the entire military health system before it generates any actual readiness benefit — worth watching as a template other institutions may copy.
items.tweetA: The Pentagon just turned a hormone level into a KPI. Annual testosterone screening for every service member over 30, TRT offered when low. Every biomarker that becomes a readiness metric eventually becomes a chronic-care budget line.
items.tweetB: A single non-fasting annual blood draw is about to generate how many false-low referrals across hundreds of thousands of troops? Multiply that by the military health system's existing clinic capacity constraints. That's the actual TRT market this policy just created.

charts.desc: No chart; policy analysis piece

items.title: Groq and Cerebras proved timing beats architecture in AI chips — here's the 4-question test for who's next
items.idea: Groq was acquired for $20B and Cerebras IPO'd above a $40B+ market cap despite both running SRAM-only preGPT architectures the author calls flawed for modern LLMs; his framework for the next trillion-dollar chip company: does it run frontier 1T+ parameter models, does it ship rack-scale, does it beat the incumbent GPU on one clear KPI, and does it land a frontier lab or hyperscaler as an anchor customer.
items.angle: This reframes AI chip investing away from best-architecture-wins toward whoever ships first with one killer number and one whale customer — a useful scorecard for the ~11 inference-chip startups racing toward rack-scale shipment in 2026-2028.
items.tweetA: Groq and Cerebras won eleven-figure outcomes with architectures arguably wrong for modern LLMs. They won because they had production silicon exactly when the market needed one KPI edge. Timing beat engineering, twice.
items.tweetB: Everyone grades AI chip startups on architecture. The actual scorecard is four questions: frontier-scale model, rack-scale shipment, one KPI beat, one whale customer. Most of the hyped names fail question four.

charts.desc: Frontier chip race scoreboard visualized by ship date across 11 companies — worth opening

items.title: Tenstorrent is first to ship a frontier AI rack in 2026 — and has zero hyperscaler customers
items.idea: Tenstorrent's Blackhole Galaxy reached general availability in April 2026 on a deliberately anti-disaggregation, anti-HBM bet (GDDR6 plus on-chip SRAM, no shared global memory, Ethernet scale-out instead of NVLink), but its named customers are neoclouds, sovereigns, and IP licensees; reported Qualcomm acquisition talks near $8-10B have reportedly gone cold.
items.angle: Tenstorrent is furthest along on the ship-date criterion from the framework above but weakest on the whale-customer criterion the same framework says actually decides who reaches gigawatt scale.
items.tweetA: Tenstorrent is the only company shipping a frontier-class AI rack in 2026. Zero hyperscaler or frontier-lab customers named. Which matters more for a trillion-dollar outcome — being first, or being chosen?
items.tweetB: Jim Keller's entire bet is no disaggregation, no HBM, one chip runs everything — the opposite of where the rest of the industry is racing. If he's right, the memory-supercycle capex thesis gets a lot more contested.

charts.desc: See frontier race scoreboard chart from the same article, item above

items.title: Samsung overtook Nvidia as the world's most profitable company — and its phone division just posted its first loss ever
items.idea: Samsung's Q2 2026 revenue rose 129.3% y/y and operating profit rose 1,811.9% y/y, making it the most profitable company in the world, but the stock still fell 6.25% on a 0.7% revenue miss driven by the Mobile eXperience division's first-ever quarterly loss since its 2021 founding, as rising memory costs erode Samsung's own smartphone margins.
items.angle: If a vertically integrated memory maker can't absorb its own DRAM inflation without its phone business losing money, the read-through for Apple, Microsoft, Google, and Amazon hardware margins into earnings season is the trade to watch.
items.tweetA: Samsung's memory business grew profit 1,812% y/y. Its phone division just posted its first loss ever, on the same memory costs. A 0.7% revenue miss wiped 6.25% off the stock in a day. Imagine that reaction landing at Apple or Microsoft instead.
items.tweetB: The market will read the next hyperscaler hardware miss as proof AI spending is failing. Samsung just showed you the actual mechanism: too much AI memory demand cannibalizing its own consumer hardware margins. Same input, opposite headline.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the Samsung segment revenue/profit breakdown

items.title: Memory will be 73% of hyperscaler AI capex by 2027, per JPMorgan
items.idea: JPMorgan estimates memory's share of cloud-service-provider capex rising from an already-large 35-50% today to 52% in 2026 and 73% in 2027 — meaning within 18 months, nearly three-quarters of hyperscaler AI infrastructure spending goes to memory chips, not compute.
items.angle: Every AI capex headline number needs a haircut for memory inflation — the GPU story is increasingly a memory story wearing a GPU's name tag.
items.tweetA: 73% of hyperscaler AI capex will be memory by 2027, per JPMorgan. Say 'AI capex' out loud one more time like it's mostly buying GPUs.
items.tweetB: 35% to 73% memory share of capex in under two years. That's not a supply cycle, that's a structural repricing of what building AI infrastructure actually costs.

charts.desc: No chart in email; cites JPMorgan estimate directly

items.title: A second wave of US strikes on Iran landed after the closing bell
items.idea: US Central Command announced a second wave of strikes against Iran in post-market trade, pushing WTI further up after the close; Marex's Ryan Fitzmaurice notes the oil market has much less spare-capacity cushion available to absorb a disruption than in prior Middle East flare-ups because the readily available emergency barrels have already been drawn down.
items.angle: A second strike wave hitting after today's cool-CPI, soft-landing rally is exactly the kind of headline that can undo a soft narrative overnight — worth checking oil futures before Thursday's open.
items.tweetA: A second wave of US strikes on Iran was announced after the close. Marex says the oil market has no emergency-barrel cushion left this time. Today's soft-CPI rally story might not survive the overnight session.
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether the Fed hikes off sticky inflation. The bigger tail risk landed after the bell: a second strike wave hitting an oil market that's already used up its spare capacity.

charts.desc: Charts inside the full markets update — worth opening for the oil, dollar and rates dashboard

items.title: The Beige Book that justifies a Fed hold was collected before Hormuz reopened and shut again
items.idea: The July Beige Book, covering observations through July 6, showed growth broadening to all Districts but one, employment gains jumping from just one District to five, and price pressure cooling 'the same or slower' everywhere for the first time since the Middle East conflict began — but the report itself flags that the cooling traces largely to a since-broken interim peace deal and a reopened Strait of Hormuz.
items.angle: The Fed's own survey data supporting a hold is already stale, the same problem sitting underneath every CPI- and PPI-driven hike-odds move covered elsewhere in this window.
items.tweetA: Beige Book shows employment gains jumping from one District to five. Great data — collected through July 6, before Hormuz reopened and then shut again. The Fed is set to hold on a survey that's already out of date.
items.tweetB: Same theme running through PCE, CPI, PPI and now the Beige Book: all of it reflects a world where Hormuz was calm. It isn't calm anymore. Every 'the Fed doesn't need to hike' data point released this week has a use-by date of about a week ago.

charts.desc: No chart; Fed survey text release

items.title: BlackRock added $192 billion of cash in a single quarter
items.idea: BlackRock crossed $15.3 trillion in assets under management, adding roughly $192B of cash in a single quarter on higher fees, while Morgan Stanley hauled in about $148B of wealth assets off the SpaceX-led IPO frenzy — flows the author says are larger than even the dot-com wave, with the caveat that this scale of money flooding markets typically shows up at the latest stage of a tech cycle.
items.angle: Pair this with the SpaceX bond stress covered elsewhere this window: record wealth-management inflows off an IPO that's already trading below its own bond-implied risk premium is exactly the late-cycle mismatch worth tracking.
items.tweetA: BlackRock added $192 billion of cash in ONE quarter. Morgan Stanley pulled in $148 billion off IPO frenzy. Meanwhile the flagship IPO of that frenzy, SpaceX, is trading below its issue price with its bonds yielding 7.5%. The money desk and the risk desk aren't agreeing right now.
items.tweetB: Every tech-wave newsletter this week compares today's fee bonanza favorably to dot-com. That comparison is the warning, not the reassurance — dot-com's best fee quarters were also its best marketing material, right before the top.

charts.desc: No charts; video/podcast newsletter format

items.title: Stripe just offered $53 billion for PayPal
items.idea: Stripe, together with Advent, has offered to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion, an explicitly AI-driven bid framed around accelerating AI into global payment systems — PayPal was the original late-1990s internet payments company, one of whose co-founders was Elon Musk.
items.angle: A private payments company attempting to take a public incumbent private at this scale, mid-AI-cycle, is the kind of M&A-wave marker that usually shows up late in a tech cycle, and it's a live name-check for anyone still holding PayPal stock waiting on a value re-rate.
items.tweetA: Stripe just bid $53 billion to take PayPal private. The company that invented online payments getting bought by the company that made it boring to compete with it. $PYPL
items.tweetB: PayPal's co-founder was Elon Musk. Now Musk's SpaceX is trading below its IPO price while Stripe tries to buy PayPal outright. Two threads of the same 1990s payments story colliding in the same week.

charts.desc: No chart; video/podcast newsletter format

items.title: ASML is raising prices despite TSMC's resistance
items.idea: ASML is raising chip-equipment prices even against pushback from TSMC, which has spent two years absorbing industry cost increases in what the author calls its 'Fed of the AI tech wave' role; ASML's lithography machines run into the hundreds of millions of dollars each, are built from 100-200+ suppliers that mostly can't scale, and more than 30% of ASML's system shipments still go into China, keeping it 2-3 years behind on EUV-made chips.
items.angle: If ASML's pricing power breaks TSMC's cost-absorption role, that repricing flows straight through to every chip customer's margins — a second, quieter input-cost pressure sitting right alongside the memory story.
items.tweetA: TSMC has spent two years playing 'Fed of the chip economy' — absorbing cost hikes so customers never feel them. ASML just tested whether that role survives real scarcity. $ASML $TSM
items.tweetB: 100-200+ suppliers, unit volumes in the hundreds not millions, demand accelerating through the decade. That's a monopoly-adjacent pricing setup. ASML raising prices into TSMC's resistance is the tell that even fabs can't out-negotiate physics.

charts.desc: No chart; video/podcast newsletter format

items.title: The market just started separating AI check-writers from check-receivers
items.idea: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rose roughly 83% YTD before falling 2.1% in a single session even as the S&P 500 held its gains on the same cool CPI/PPI data — the piece frames this as capital rotating out of 'the hottest room in the house,' with the market beginning to separate AI check-writers (hyperscalers funding the buildout) from check-receivers (chip and memory suppliers getting paid), after years of treating receivers as the cleaner trade.
items.angle: The check-writers-versus-receivers framing is the cleanest mental model this cycle has produced for why a good chip earnings beat can still sell off — worth re-checking which side of that ledger any AI position actually sits on.
items.tweetA: The market is splitting AI stocks into check-writers (hyperscalers funding the buildout) and check-receivers (chip/memory suppliers getting paid). For two years receivers were the 'safe' trade. That's flipping.
items.tweetB: Semis up 83% YTD, down 2.1% in a session, while the S&P holds its gains on the exact same data. That's not the AI thesis breaking — that's a crowded trade finally getting priced like one.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index chart

items.title: SpaceX's stock fell 40%. Its bond is the scarier chart.
items.idea: SpaceX fell below its $135 IPO price, nearly 40% off the June 15 peak above $220, but the more consequential move is its new $25B bond due 2056, which has fallen steadily since it began trading on June 24 toward a 7.5% yield, pushing Goldman's hyperscaler credit basket to its widest level since the basket launched in February.
items.angle: Equity dreams are optional, bond yields are a receipt — a 7.5% yield on a headline AI-infrastructure name is the market pricing financing risk into the capex supercycle for the first time this cycle, and every other hyperscaler's next bond print is now the number to watch.
items.tweetA: SpaceX's stock is down 40% from its peak. Its bond is the scarier chart: a 30-year note issued weeks ago is already yielding 7.5%. Bondholders don't buy narrative.
items.tweetB: Equity investors are still pricing the AI dream. Bondholders are now pricing who actually pays for it. A 7.5% print on brand-new hyperscaler-adjacent debt is the clearest 'show me the cash flow' signal of the year.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the SpaceX bond yield and hyperscaler credit basket chart

items.source: The Boom Daily (thedarksideoftheboom, citing Goldman Sachs)
items.title: Goldman: hyperscaler bond cover ratios are falling sharply
items.idea: Goldman Sachs says record hyperscaler debt issuance is shifting from a temporary technical headwind to a structural credit-market burden — cover ratios (bid-to-cover on new issuance) have fallen sharply, meaning investors demand more compensation for each new wave of supply, even as capex estimates keep rising and free cash flow is pushed deeper into negative territory.
items.angle: Falling cover ratios are the leading indicator, the SpaceX 7.5% yield above is the lagging confirmation — two independent data sources agreeing on the same signal is worth weighing more heavily than either alone.
items.tweetA: What happens when the best-funded companies on earth start seeing falling cover ratios on their own bonds? Goldman just flagged it. SpaceX's 7.5% yield just confirmed it. That's not one data point, that's a pattern.
items.tweetB: Two different ways to measure the same thing this week: cover ratios from Goldman, yield levels from SpaceX's bond. Both say the market wants a bigger premium to fund the AI buildout. Equity multiples haven't caught up to that yet.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the cover-ratio and FCF trend charts

items.title: 7.5 million barrels a day are shut in in the Middle East and oil is still under $80
items.idea: HFIR's tally of this week's Middle East oil disruptions: two VLCCs attacked transiting the Strait of Hormuz's Oman lane, roughly 7.5 million barrels a day of crude production shut in, active US-Iran strikes, and a closed Strait of Hormuz — yet Brent trades near $85 and WTI below $80, with sell-side reports still calling a return to normal by the end of July, two weeks away.
items.angle: This is the largest gap between headline supply disruption and headline price the author has seen — floating storage is being counted as flowing supply, and the market is pricing a resolution date that keeps not arriving.
items.tweetA: 7.5 million barrels a day shut in. Two tankers attacked in Hormuz. Active US-Iran strikes. The Strait is closed. Brent is at $85. In any other decade this would already be triple digits. Do the math on what happens if the market's assumed Hormuz-reopens-by-July-31 timeline slips again.
items.tweetB: Sell-side keeps calling for Hormuz to normalize by end of July. That deadline is two weeks away and nothing on the ground supports it. The oil market isn't wrong about the war — it's wrong about the calendar.

charts.desc: No chart; text-only market commentary
